name: Humanity for Relief and Development
mission:
THE ORGANIZATION IS A NONPROFIT
ORGANIZATION ON A MISSION TO IMPROVE THE LIVES OF INDIVIDUALS AND FAMILIES
AFFECTED BY THE WAR IN YEMEN THROUGH A RANGE OF SUSTAINABLE PROJECTS, PROVIDING
TANGIBLE RESULTS WITH A POSITIVE IMPACT.
scoreSummary: Humanity for Relief and Development, Inc. shows strong alignment and good impact, with zakat compliance (significant risk deductions applied).
zakatClaimEvidence: Your zakat contributions can be rightfully directed towards Humanity for Relief and Development, as we are a recognized zakat-eligible entity. By entrusting us with your zakat donations, you contribute to the betterment of our programs and provide much-needed assistance to those we serve. Zakat For Relief. Humanity for Relief and Development is a zakat-eligible organization. (Source: https://hrdglobal.org/zakat-eligibility/)

ui_signals_v1.recommendation_rationale: Good donor alignment with manageable risk. Evidence quality is solid but still evolving in places.

keyConcerns.headline: 81% of expenses stay in the US despite international operations
keyConcerns.detail: Organization claims foreign operations but most spending remains domestic.

components.evidence: Accepts zakat (+2); Muslim-focused organization (+2); Asnaf: fuqara (+5); Operates in Muslim-majority regions (+3); Humanitarian service (+2)
components.name: Muslim Donor Fit

components.improvement_suggestion: Expand services to underserved populations or geographies with limited nonprofit coverage.
components.name: Underserved Space

alignment.rationale: Alignment 35/50: HIGH Muslim donor fit, education global cause area

components.evidence: No beneficiary data available for cost-per-beneficiary scoring; good program ratio (≥75%) suggests reasonable delivery
components.improvement_suggestion: Publish beneficiary counts consistently so cost-effectiveness can be assessed.
components.name: Cost Per Beneficiary

components.improvement_suggestion: If reserves are above policy target, document why and publish triggers for planned deployment.
components.name: Financial Health

components.improvement_suggestion: Document a clear theory of change with an explicit causal pathway.
components.name: Theory of Change

judge_issues.message: The rationale mentions "excellent financial health" but doesn't acknowledge the "High domestic burn: 81% of expenses stay in the US despite international operations" risk, which significantly impacted the overall score through a risk deduction.
The impact rationale doesn't mention the missing theory of change, which is a significant factor in the impact score.
Website blocked by anti-bot protection: CAPTCHA_BLOCKED: challenge page (HTTP 202)
Website citation for 'has_audited_financials' uses homepage URL; deep link preferred

all_citations.claim: HRD holds a Charity Navigator score of 97.33333333333333/100 and maintains a program expense ratio of 78.1%.
The organization is zakat-eligible and follows specific policies to distribute funds to appropriate asnaf categories.
The organization reported Total Revenue of $3,669,260 and operates programs including food distribution and medical relief.

baseline_narrative.amal_score_rationale: HRD earns a solid rating due to its high Charity Navigator score of 97.33333333333333/100 and its direct service model in a high-urgency cause area. While financial health is excellent, the overall score reflects a balance between strong operational metrics and the inherent challenges of reporting impact in a conflict zone.
areas_for_improvement: Limited geographic diversification outside of Yemen-focused relief
Opportunity to provide more granular cost-per-beneficiary data for impact assessment

dimension_explanations.alignment: HRD shows high alignment for Muslim donors through its verified Zakat-eligible status and focus on global education and relief in high-urgency regions [2]. The organization's commitment to asnaf categories ensures that zakat contributions are handled with religious integrity [2].
dimension_explanations.impact: The organization demonstrates high impact through direct service programs, including medical relief and food security initiatives, supported by a Total Revenue of $3,669,260 [1][3]. Its financial health is underscored by a 78.1% program expense Ratio, indicating that the majority of funds directly support its mission in Yemen [1].

baseline_narrative.headline: Humanity for Relief and Development provides critical humanitarian aid and sustainable support to families affected by the ongoing crisis in Yemen.
strengths: High financial efficiency with a program expense ratio of 78.1%
Strong fundraising efficiency, spending only $0.08 to raise every $1
Robust liquidity with 15.0 months of working capital available
baseline_narrative.summary: Humanity for Relief and Development (HRD) is a non-profit organization dedicated to improving the lives of individuals and families in Yemen through food distribution, medical relief, and orphanage support [1][3]. The organization maintains a high level of financial transparency and is recognized as zakat-eligible, ensuring that donations reach those in need according to Islamic principles [2].

all_citations.quote: HRD ensures all Zakat donations are distributed to eligible asnaf.

areas_for_improvement.context: While HRD tracks outcomes, it currently lacks <cite id="1">consistent cost-per-beneficiary data</cite>, making it difficult to assess the relative efficiency of its various interventions.
The organization currently lacks a <cite id="1">documented theory of change</cite>, which would help donors understand the long-term causal pathway of its humanitarian programs.

bbb_assessment.summary: Meets all 20 BBB Wise Giving Alliance Standards for Charity Accountability

case_against.mitigation_notes: The organization maintains high liquidity to offset revenue volatility and is actively tracking six outcome metrics via Candid.
risk_factors: <cite id="3">3-year revenue CAGR of -1.9%</cite> indicates stagnant or slightly declining funding.
<cite id="1">Lack of explicit theory of change</cite> documentation.
case_against.summary: The primary concerns involve <cite id="3">negative revenue growth</cite> and high domestic spending relative to international delivery.

confidence.notes: Strong financial and accountability data available.

known_gaps: No formal theory of change document

alignment.explanation: HRD is a strong fit for Muslim donors as it is <cite id="2">zakat-eligible</cite> and serves the <cite id="2">fuqara and masakin</cite> categories in high-need areas like Yemen.
alignment.improvement: The charity could improve alignment by <cite id="1">expanding services to more underserved geographies</cite> beyond its current primary focus areas.

credibility.explanation: Donors can trust HRD due to its <cite id="1">Platinum Seal of Transparency</cite> and its status of meeting <cite id="1">all 20 BBB Standards for Charity Accountability</cite>. The board consists of <cite id="3">5 members</cite> with a clear conflict of interest policy in place.
credibility.improvement: To further enhance credibility, the organization could publish more granular <cite id="1">independent evaluation documents</cite> to verify program outcomes.

impact.explanation: The charity shows high efficiency with an <cite id="3">78.1% program expense ratio</cite> and <cite id="1">15.0 months of working capital</cite>, ensuring it can sustain operations during funding fluctuations.
impact.improvement: Impact measurement would benefit from <cite id="1">publishing consistent beneficiary counts</cite> to allow for cost-effectiveness analysis.

donor_fit_matrix.evidence_rigor: Grade C - Verified outcomes with 6 metrics tracked

grantmaking_profile.grant_strategy: Distributes the majority of grants to foreign implementing partners for direct relief.

rich_narrative.headline: Humanity for Relief and Development provides critical humanitarian aid and sustainable support to families affected by the ongoing crisis in Yemen.

ideal_donor_profile.best_for_summary: Best for donors who want to fulfill zakat obligations by supporting an established, highly transparent organization providing direct humanitarian aid in Yemen.
donor_motivations: Want to ensure high financial accountability for their zakat
Prioritize relief for the humanitarian crisis in Yemen
Value charities with high liquidity and low fundraising costs
Prefer organizations with a Platinum level of transparency
giving_considerations: The organization is currently experiencing a slight decline in revenue growth
Most impact data is self-reported rather than independently evaluated
Operations are highly concentrated in a few geographic regions
ideal_donor_profile.not_ideal_for: Donors seeking high-growth organizations or those who require rigorous third-party academic impact evaluations (RCTs).

impact_evidence.evidence_grade_explanation: Grade C reflects that the organization tracks <cite id="1">6 outcome metrics</cite> and shares feedback practices, but lacks a formal theory of change or external RCTs.
impact_evidence.theory_of_change_summary: HRD seeks to empower individuals in conflict zones by providing access to free food and livelihood development to break the cycle of poverty.
impact_evidence.why_evidence_matters: Tracking outcomes ensures that food and medical distributions are effectively reaching the most vulnerable populations in Yemen.

long_term_outlook.room_for_funding_explanation: The ongoing crisis in Yemen creates a massive funding gap that HRD is well-positioned to address if revenue growth stabilizes.

peer_comparison.differentiator: HRD maintains a <cite id="1">97.97.33333333333333/100 from Charity Navigator score</cite>, which is significantly higher than many peers of similar size.

similar_organizations.differentiator: Similar focus on orphan care and humanitarian relief

strengths.detail: The charity maintains an <cite id="1">overall score of 97.97.33333333333333/100 from Charity Navigator</cite>, driven by perfect scores in Culture and Leadership.
HRD directs <cite id="3">78.1% of its total expenses</cite> toward direct program services, exceeding the 75% industry standard.
The organization holds <cite id="1">15.0 months of working capital</cite>, providing a significant safety net for emergency operations.

strengths_deep_dive: Humanity for Relief and Development demonstrates a high level of operational efficiency by maintaining a <cite id="1">fundraising efficiency of $0.08 per $1 raised</cite>. This lean approach allows more resources to be funneled into its core mission areas, such as food security and orphan care. Furthermore, the organization has achieved <cite id="1">perfect 100/100 scores in Culture and Leadership</cite> beacons from Charity Navigator, indicating a healthy internal organizational environment. Their commitment to transparency is evidenced by meeting <cite id="1">all 20 BBB Wise Giving Alliance Standards</cite> for charity accountability, which includes rigorous checks on governance and financial reporting.
rich_narrative.summary: Humanity for Relief and Development (HRD) operates as a critical lifeline for communities in Yemen, focusing on food security, medical aid, and sustainable livelihood development. The organization reported <cite id="3">$3,669,260 in total revenue</cite> for the 2024 tax year, maintaining a strong focus on direct service delivery. HRD is distinguished by its <cite id="1">overall score of 97.97.33333333333333/100 from Charity Navigator</cite>, which reflects high standards of accountability and transparency. While the charity is relatively young, it has quickly established a significant footprint in Yemen and Pakistan. The organization manages to keep overhead low, ensuring that <cite id="3">78.1% of expenses goes to programs</cite>. This efficiency is further highlighted by its fundraising model, where it spends only <cite id="1">$0.08 to raise every $1</cite>. HRD also holds a <cite id="1">Platinum Seal of Transparency from Candid</cite>, the highest level of disclosure available. For Muslim donors, the charity provides a clear framework for religious giving, as it is <cite id="2">verified as zakat-eligible</cite> and serves specific categories such as the poor (fuqara) and needy (masakin). Despite its strong financial metrics, the organization faces a <cite id="3">3-year revenue CAGR of -1.9%</cite>, suggesting a need for more diversified funding streams to sustain its long-term growth.
